itineraries.title: Complete Iceland: Highlands, Glaciers & Fjords in 17 Days
itineraries.subtitle: The ultimate Icelandic adventure combining the Ring Road with remote Highlands - Landmannalaugar, Kerlingarfjöll, Vatnajökull glaciers and eastern fjords
itineraries.notes: Summer-only itinerary (late June - early September). 4x4 mandatory for days 3-5 (Highlands F-roads). Nights at Landmannalaugar are in campsites/mountain huts - no hotels, no shops. Book camping in advance. Fill up on fuel before each Highlands section. /// DIFFERENCE FROM THE 12-DAY VERSION: This itinerary includes 2 days in the Highlands (Landmannalaugar, rhyolite mountains, natural hot spring) + Seyðisfjörður (artists' village) + Húsavík (whale watching) as separate stops. In return, it requires a 4x4 and is only accessible in summer (late June - early September). The 12-day circuit covers the same major sites but stays on paved roads with a standard vehicle.

visits.text: Arrive at Keflavík airport and head to Reykjavik to explore the city center: Hallgrímskirkja (245 ft tall, 1945-1986, facade inspired by basalt columns), Harpa Concert Hall (714 glass panels, Mies van der Rohe Award 2013), the Old Harbour, and Laugavegur shopping street. Climb the church tower for the panorama - it's the best view of the city.
If you arrive early, visit the National Museum (Þjóðminjasafn) for an introduction to Iceland's Viking history. The centerpiece is a 13th-century church door carved with dragons.

activities.text: Take your first Icelandic dip in a neighborhood geothermal pool. Vesturbæjarlaug is less touristy than the big ones - water at 100°F, local atmosphere.

visits.text: Golden Circle day. Start with Þingvellir: UNESCO-listed since 2004, this is where the Althing was founded in 930 - the world's oldest parliament. You're walking through the rift between the Eurasian and North American tectonic plates, which are drifting apart at 0.8 inches per year.

At Haukadalur, Strokkur geyser erupts every 5-10 minutes, shooting 50-65 feet into the air. Its neighbor Geysir - the one that gave its name to the phenomenon - is now almost dormant. Position yourself downwind to avoid scalding spray.
Gullfoss: the Hvítá river plunges in two tiers, 105 feet into a 230-foot-deep canyon. On clear days, a permanent rainbow forms in the spray. Take the path down to get closest to the lower falls.

On your way back to Selfoss, detour to Kerið crater: a 3,000-year-old volcanic crater filled with a blue-green lake 45 feet deep. The contrast between the red crater rock and turquoise water is striking.

activities.text: In the evening, explore the small town of Selfoss, a practical supply point. If you want a more exclusive bath, the Secret Lagoon in Flúðir is a natural hot spring at 100-104°F in a rural setting - Iceland's oldest pool, open since 1891. Important: Fill up in Selfoss or Hella - these are the last gas stations before the Highlands F-roads.

visits.text: Head into the Highlands via Route 32 then F208 north. First stop: Hjálparfoss, a twin waterfall tumbling into a pool surrounded by basalt columns. Then Háifoss, one of Iceland's tallest waterfalls (400 ft), visible from a viewpoint right on the cliff edge - vertigo guaranteed.

Arrive at Landmannalaugar by midday. This geothermal site in the heart of the Fjallabak Nature Reserve is surrounded by rhyolite mountains in mind-bending colors - yellow, green, pink, red, orange. The Laugahraun lava field, formed during a 1477 eruption, borders the campsite. This is the starting point for the famous Laugavegur trek (34 miles to Þórsmörk).

activities.text: Bathe in Landmannalaugar's natural hot spring - a hot stream mixes with a cold stream at the foot of the lava field, creating a natural pool at perfect temperature. Free and open to all. Warning: The only accommodation is the mountain hut and campsite (no hotels). Bring your own food - there are no shops here.

visits.text: Hiking day. The Brennisteinsalda loop ('sulphur wave') is the most popular: this mountain is considered Iceland's most colorful - layers of sulphur yellow, moss green, iron red, and rhyolite pink. The loop is about 3.7 miles and takes 2-3 hours.

For the more athletic, Bláhnjúkur ('blue peak', 3,094 ft) offers 360° panoramas of the Highlands from its summit. The ascent is steep (about 1 hour) on black and blue volcanic ash - the contrast with the rhyolite mountains below is spectacular.

activities.text: If you have a 4x4 and time, detour to Ljótipollur, a blood-red crater filled with intensely blue water - 20 minutes on the track. The contrast is surreal. In the evening, return to the hot springs to soothe your muscles after hiking.

visits.text: Drive south via F208 then return to the Ring Road. Stop at Fjaðrárgljúfur canyon: 330 feet deep and 1.2 miles long, carved 9,000 years ago by glacial erosion. A path runs along the rim with viewing platforms. The site had to temporarily close after a Justin Bieber music video brought too many visitors.

In Kirkjubæjarklaustur (which Icelanders shorten to 'Klaustur'), visit Kirkjugólf, the 'church floor' - a flat surface of hexagonal basalt columns that looks like natural tiling. Medieval monks thought it was the floor of a vanished church. Systrafoss waterfall (82 ft) is a short walk away.

activities.text: Klaustur is a good supply point - gas station, grocery store, restaurants. Take advantage because between here and Höfn, services are very scarce. If you have a robust 4x4, the Laki craters (Lakagígar) are accessible on a day trip from Klaustur - 130 craters aligned over 15.5 miles, remnants of the 1783 eruption that killed a quarter of Iceland's population and affected the global climate.

visits.text: At Skaftafell, take the trail to Svartifoss (3.4 miles round trip, 394 ft elevation gain). This 66-foot waterfall tumbles in front of an amphitheater of black basalt columns - it inspired the architect of Hallgrímskirkja church.

Jökulsárlón: this 6.9-square-mile lagoon didn't exist before 1935. At 932 feet deep (Iceland's deepest lake), it's filled with blue, white, and black icebergs made from ice over 1,000 years old. Walk along the west shore to escape the crowds.
Diamond Beach, just across Route 1: wave-polished icebergs wash up on the black sand. Come at sunset for the best colors.

activities.text: Operators offer zodiac tours on the lagoon to navigate between icebergs and approach the glacier front. Book ahead in summer.

visits.text: Morning at Höfn harbor, the capital of humar (Icelandic langoustine). The docks offer stunning views of Vatnajökull in clear weather - Europe's largest ice cap appears to tumble straight into the sea. The Vatnajökull National Park visitor center is worth a stop.

Detour to Fjallsárlón, a smaller and much less crowded glacier lagoon than Jökulsárlón. The Fjallsjökull glacier front plunges directly into it - you'll likely be alone facing the icebergs and the cracking ice.

activities.text: Operators offer guided glacier hikes (crampons provided). It's your chance to walk on the Vatnajökull ice, among blue crevasses and glacial moulins. Allow 3-4 hours for a full excursion.

visits.text: Drive along the East Fjords, one of Iceland's least visited regions. In Djúpivogur (population 450), giant stone eggs line the harbor - reproductions of eggs from 34 local bird species.
The road winds between deep fjords and fishing villages. Every turn offers a new panorama. Make photo stops - the East Fjords are Iceland's best-kept secret.
At Egilsstaðir, the hike to Fardagafoss (2.5 miles round trip, 30-minute climb) leads to a waterfall in a rocky amphitheater with valley views.

activities.text: Walk along Lake Lagarfljót (15.5 miles long, 367 feet deep), home according to legend to the Lagarfljótsormur, a giant serpent cousin of the Loch Ness monster. In 2012, a video showing a serpentine shape went viral worldwide.

visits.text: Before leaving the area, detour to Hengifoss, Iceland's third-highest waterfall (420 ft). The 1.5-mile trail from the parking lot follows Lake Lagarfljót and passes Litlanesfoss first, framed by basalt columns. The red clay layers striping the cliff around Hengifoss are spectacular. Allow 1.5 hours round trip.

The 16.8-mile road between Egilsstaðir and Seyðisfjörður crosses a mountain pass before plunging toward the fjord - one of Iceland's most beautiful descents. Along the way, a series of waterfalls lines the road.

Seyðisfjörður is the most beautiful village in the East Fjords. At the head of the fjord, colorful houses line a rainbow-paved path leading to Bláa kirkjan, the little blue church. The village attracts artists and musicians - several galleries and workshops dot the main street. It's also the ferry arrival port from Denmark.

activities.text: Several hiking trails start from the village. The trail to Gufufoss waterfall (1.2 miles) offers a plunging view of the fjord and village below. If you have the full day, the coastal trail to Skálanes (about 3 miles) crosses wild landscapes with chances of seeing seals and bird colonies.

visits.text: Drive north. Dettifoss first: 144 ft high, 328 ft wide, average flow of 7,063 cubic feet per second - Europe's most powerful waterfall. You hear the roar before you see it. Approach from the west bank (Route 862, paved). Opening scene of Prometheus.

18.6 miles north, Ásbyrgi: a horseshoe canyon 2.2 miles long, 0.6 miles wide, with 328-foot cliffs. According to mythology, it's the hoofprint of Sleipnir, Odin's horse. A birch forest thrives at the bottom.
Arrive at Mývatn: detour to Grjótagjá cave (hot water lake, Game of Thrones filming location, bathing forbidden >122°F).

activities.text: Drive around the lake (22.4 miles) to scout tomorrow's sites. The midge lake hosts 13 species of nesting ducks in summer.

visits.text: Dimmuborgir ('dark castles'): 2,300-year-old lava field, pillars, arches and caves sculpted by steam. The Kirkjan cave resembles a natural Gothic cathedral. Game of Thrones filming location.
Hverfjall crater: 0.6 miles in diameter, 460 ft deep, formed 2,500 years ago. Steep climb (820 ft, 20-30 min) but exceptional 360° panorama. Rim circuit in 1 hour.
Hverir (Námaskarð): geothermal field - mud pots, fumaroles, sulphur smell, ochre to red ground. You'd think you were on Mars. Free, right by Route 1. Stay on the trails - the ground is scalding.
Krafla volcano: Víti crater ('hell') holds a blue-green lake formed in 1724. At Leirhnjúkur, still-steaming lava flows date from the 1975-1984 eruptions.

activities.text: Bathe at Mývatn Nature Baths (Jarðböðin): milky blue-green water between 97-104°F, cheaper and less crowded than the Blue Lagoon. Open until midnight in summer - in June, bathe under the midnight sun.

visits.text: Húsavík is Iceland's whale-watching capital. The Whale Museum (Hvalasafnið) is Iceland's most comprehensive - it displays several complete skeletons, including an 82-foot blue whale. The Eurovision Song Contest movie (2020) with Will Ferrell was partly filmed here.
Húsavík Church (Húsavíkurkirkja), built in 1907 from Norwegian timber, is one of the country's most charming with its red facade and pointed steeple facing the harbor.

activities.text: Operators offer whale-watching excursions (2-3h). Skjálfandi Bay is one of the world's best spots: humpback whales, minke whales, white-beaked dolphins, and sometimes blue whales. The observation rate approaches 99% in summer.

End the day at GeoSea baths, perched on a cliff above the harbor. The geothermal seawater at 100-104°F comes from a 6,560-foot-deep borehole. The view of the bay, mountains, and possibly whales from the pool is remarkable.

visits.text: Goðafoss en route: 98 ft wide, 39 ft high, 'waterfall of the gods'. In the year 1000, chieftain Þorgeir allegedly threw his statues of Norse gods into it when Iceland adopted Christianity. East bank for the best view.

Akureyri (pop. 19,000): Akureyrarkirkja (same architect as Hallgrímskirkja, stained glass from Coventry), Lystigarðurinn botanical gardens (the world's northernmost, 7,000 species, free admission), heart-shaped traffic lights (2008 initiative).

activities.text: Akureyri municipal swimming pool: several heated outdoor pools, slides, steam room. The quintessential Icelandic social institution - locals gather here to chat.

visits.text: Long drive west. Stop at Borgarnes for the Settlement Center (Viking settlement, Saga of Egill). Then Hraunfossar waterfalls - dozens of streams gushing from a lava field over 0.6 miles.
Arrive in Stykkishólmur late in the day. Climb to the Súgandisey lighthouse for views of Breiðafjörður fjord and its thousands of islets.

activities.text: Explore Stykkishólmur's colorful harbor. If you still have energy, try the local mussels - the town's specialty.

visits.text: Kirkjufell (1,519 ft): Iceland's most photographed mountain, with Kirkjufellsfoss waterfall in the foreground. The 'arrowhead mountain' from Game of Thrones. Come early or late to avoid crowds.
Arnarstapi-Hellnar coastal trail (1.5 miles, 45 min): arches, sea caves, basalt columns. The Gatklettur arch at Arnarstapi is spectacular.
Djúpalónssandur: black pebble beach, 4 fishermen's lifting stones (51-339 lbs), wreckage of a trawler that sank in 1948.
Búðakirkja: black church from 1848 in the middle of a mossy lava field. The Snæfellsjökull glacier (4,744 ft, 'Journey to the Center of the Earth' by Jules Verne) dominates the peninsula.

activities.text: If weather permits, operators offer excursions on Snæfellsjökull glacier. The guided ascent takes 4-5 hours round trip. Book in advance.

visits.text: Return to Reykjavik. Perlan: glass dome on geothermal tanks, 330-foot artificial ice cave, planetarium, 360° terrace. A good summary of Icelandic nature if you still have questions after 16 days on the island.
Grandi district: maritime museum, galleries, food court. It's Reykjavik's liveliest neighborhood for dining and browsing.

activities.text: Last evening: geothermal baths by the sea a few minutes from downtown. Water at 100°F facing the North Atlantic with views of Mount Esja. A memorable way to conclude 17 days in Iceland.

visits.text: Departure day. If your flight is in the evening, spend the morning exploring the Reykjanes peninsula. The Gunnuhver geothermal field, 20 minutes from the airport, is a site of fumaroles and boiling mud pots - free and uncrowded. The Reykjanesviti lighthouse, Iceland's oldest (1878), is right next door.

activities.text: The Blue Lagoon (or Sky Lagoon, more recent) is between the airport and Reykjavik. If you haven't stopped yet, now's the time - book ahead as time slots fill up quickly. The milky blue water at 100-104°F is rich in silica and minerals. Perfect for relaxing before a long flight.

seo.h1_title: Complete Iceland: Highlands, Glaciers & Fjords in 17 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: The ultimate Iceland adventure for those who want to see it all - including what most visitors miss. This 17-day itinerary combines the Ring Road, the wild Highlands (Landmannalaugar and its multicolored rhyolite mountains), the Vatnajökull glaciers, the East Fjords, the Mývatn volcanic region, whale watching in Húsavík, and the Snæfellsnes peninsula.
seo.seo_hook: Multicolored rhyolite mountains at Landmannalaugar, thousand-year-old icebergs at Jökulsárlón, whale watching in Húsavík, and Jules Verne's glacier at Snæfellsnes.
featured_list: Landmannalaugar: mind-bending rhyolite mountains in the heart of the Highlands
Húsavík: whale-watching capital with 99% observation rate in summer
Snæfellsnes: peninsula crowned by Jules Verne's glacier and Kirkjufell mountain

places_taglines.IS::reykjavik: where street art meets geothermal pools under the midnight sun
places_taglines.IS::selfoss: gateway town at the crossroads of the Golden Circle and Highlands
places_taglines.IS::landmannalaugar: geothermal oasis surrounded by multicolored peaks in the wild Highlands
places_taglines.IS::kirkjubaejarklaustur: between volcanic craters and Europe's mightiest glacier
places_taglines.IS::hofn: langoustine harbor facing Europe's largest ice cap
places_taglines.IS::egilsstadir: gateway to the East Fjords and the legendary serpent lake
places_taglines.IS::seydisfjordur: where rainbow streets meet blue churches at the fjord's end
places_taglines.IS::myvatn: volcanic wonderland of craters, fumaroles and steaming earth
places_taglines.IS::husavik: where humpback whales breach under the midnight sun
places_taglines.IS::akureyri: northern capital with heart-shaped traffic lights and midnight golf
places_taglines.IS::stykkisholmur: colorful harbor town guarding the gateway to Snæfellsnes
places_taglines.IS::keflavik: where lava fields meet the Atlantic at Iceland's edge

highlights: Landmannalaugar: multicolored rhyolite mountains and natural hot spring (F-roads, 4x4 required)
Jökulsárlón: 932-foot-deep lagoon with 1,000-year-old icebergs and Diamond Beach
Húsavík: 99% whale observation rate in summer
Snæfellsjökull: Jules Verne's glacier rising to 4,744 ft

itineraries.title: Iceland Road Trip: The Ring Road in 12 Days
itineraries.subtitle: Complete circuit around the island via the Ring Road, from Reykjavik to the eastern fjords through the southern glaciers and northern volcanoes
itineraries.notes: Route 1 (Ring Road) entirely paved and passable year-round. In winter, some secondary roads (Dettifoss, Tröllaskagi) may be closed. Standard vehicle sufficient for this route - no 4x4 needed. /// DIFFERENCE WITH THE 17-DAY VERSION: this itinerary stays on the paved Route 1 (no 4x4 needed). The 17-day circuit adds 2 days in the Highlands (Landmannalaugar, F-roads) + Seyðisfjörður + Húsavík as separate stops. If you're hesitating: choose the 12-day for a comfortable first trip, the 17-day if you're a hiker wanting to venture off the beaten path.

visits.text: You'll spot Hallgrímskirkja from afar - this 74.5-meter concrete church dominates all of Reykjavik from atop Skólavörðuhæð hill. Designed by Guðjón Samúelsson in 1937, it took 41 years to complete (1945-1986), financed solely by parishioner donations. Its facade echoes the basalt columns found throughout Iceland. Take the elevator to the top of the tower: the 360° view over the city and Faxaflói bay is the best in the capital. In front of the church stands the statue of Leif Eriksson - gifted by the United States in 1930 for the thousandth anniversary of the Althing.

Walk down Skólavörðustígur street toward the harbor until you reach Harpa, the concert hall inaugurated in 2011 after a troubled construction interrupted by the 2008 financial crisis. The south facade features 714 glass panels designed by artist Ólafur Elíasson - each panel unique. The building won the Mies van der Rohe Award in 2013. Entry is free: push through the doors to admire the play of light in the lobby, especially late in the day when sunlight strikes the windows.

Continue along the waterfront eastward to reach the Sun Voyager (Sólfarið), this stainless steel sculpture resembling a Viking longship. Installed in 1990, it faces Mount Esja across the bay. It's one of Reykjavik's most iconic photo spots - come late in the day for golden light on the water.

Head back toward the center via Laugavegur, Reykjavik's main shopping street. Take time to browse Icelandic design shops, thrift stores, and cafés. If you still have energy, detour to Lake Tjörnin where locals feed swans and ducks - in winter, the lake freezes over and residents skate on it.

activities.text: In the evening, head down to the Old Harbour to explore the docks where fishing boats share space with whale-watching vessels. Several seafood restaurants line the harbor - order the plokkfiskur, a traditional Icelandic fish casserole. If you're here in summer, the sun barely sets: enjoy the evening light bathing the bay.

visits.text: Marathon day - plan an early departure (before 8am) to see everything. Early morning start for the Golden Circle. First stop: Þingvellir National Park, UNESCO-listed since 2004. This is where Vikings founded the Althing in 930 - the world's oldest parliament still in existence. You'll literally walk between the Eurasian and North American tectonic plates, through a rift valley widening by 2 cm per year. The main trail through Almannagjá gorge runs about 1 km - allow 30 to 45 minutes.

Head to Haukadalur, the geothermal area where the Strokkur geyser erupts every 5 to 10 minutes to a height of 15 to 20 meters. Its neighbor Geysir, which gave its name to the phenomenon in all languages, is now nearly dormant. Position yourself opposite the wind to avoid spray - and don't get too close, the water reaches nearly 100°C.

Third stop on the Golden Circle: Gullfoss, the 'golden falls.' The Hvítá river plunges in two tiers down 32 meters into a 70-meter-deep canyon. On clear days, the spray creates a permanent rainbow. Take the path descending closest to the lower falls - you'll get soaked, but the spectacle is worth it. The site's history is equally remarkable: in the early 20th century, Sigríður Tómasdóttir threatened to throw herself into the falls to prevent a hydroelectric dam project.

Descending toward the south coast, stop at Seljalandsfoss. This 65-meter waterfall has a unique feature: a trail allows you to walk behind the curtain of water. Bring waterproof clothing - you'll get drenched. The spectacle is particularly beautiful at sunset when light shines through the water veil. Just nearby, don't miss Gljúfrabúi, a 40-meter waterfall hidden in a narrow canyon - you must wade through a stream to reach it.

Last waterfall of the day: Skógafoss, 30 km further. At 25 meters wide and 60 meters high, it's a perfect wall of water. On sunny days, a double rainbow forms in the spray. Climb the 527 steps of the lateral staircase to reach the viewpoint: the vista plunges across the coastal plain to the ocean and Westman Islands. According to legend, the first Viking settler Þrasi hid a chest filled with gold behind the waterfall - a ring from the chest is now displayed at the Skógar folk museum next door.

activities.text: If the day is long in summer (the sun doesn't set before 11pm in June), make a detour to the Sólheimasandur plane wreck. This US Navy DC-3 crashed on the black sand beach in 1973 - the entire crew survived. The fuselage has lain there since, battered by wind. Allow 45 minutes of walking from the parking area on a flat but windy trail.

visits.text: Reynisfjara is probably Iceland's most spectacular beach. The sand is deep black - volcanic ash from nearby Katla - and the hexagonal basalt columns at the foot of Reynisfjall mountain form a sort of giant organ 20 meters high. You can enter Hálsanefshellir cave at low tide, but be extremely careful: 'sneaker waves' have caused several deaths here. Never turn your back to the ocean and stay at least 30 meters from the water.

Offshore, the Reynisdrangar rock stacks rise from the Atlantic. According to Icelandic folklore, they're trolls petrified by the rising sun while attempting to drag a ship to shore. The site served as a filming location for Game of Thrones and Star Wars. If you're here in summer (May-August), watch for puffins nesting on the cliffs and stacks - they return late in the day.

Take the road to Dyrhólaey, the promontory west of Vík. This 120-meter-high natural arch juts into the sea - boats could pass beneath it in the past. From the summit, views sweep the entire south coast, from Reynisfjara to the Mýrdalsjökull and Eyjafjallajökull glaciers. In summer, the area closes in the evening to protect nesting puffins.

Finish at the village of Vík í Mýrdal, the southernmost settlement in mainland Iceland with 300 residents. Climb to the little red church Víkurkirkja overlooking the village - from here you'll have the best view of the Reynisdrangar and the black beach below. The village lives under threat from Katla, a subglacial volcano whose last major eruption dates to 1918.

activities.text: For hiking enthusiasts, a walk along the Reynisfjall ridge connects Reynisfjara beach to Vík village from above. The trail offers vertiginous panoramas of the coast on both sides. Bring hiking boots and a windbreaker - wind blows hard on the ridge.

visits.text: Leaving Vík eastward, stop at Fjaðrárgljúfur canyon, 6 km off Route 1. This canyon - 100 meters deep and 2 km long - was carved by glacial erosion about 9,000 years ago. A marked trail follows the rim with several viewing platforms. If the site seems familiar, there's a reason: Justin Bieber shot a music video here in 2015, causing such an influx of visitors that the site had to close temporarily for regeneration.

Continue to Skaftafell in Vatnajökull National Park. From there, take the hiking trail to Svartifoss, the 'black falls.' At 20 meters high, it tumbles before an amphitheater of dark basalt columns - this waterfall inspired the architect of Hallgrímskirkja. The trail runs about 5.5 km round trip with 120 meters elevation gain. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours.

Jökulsárlón is the highlight of the day. This 18 km² glacial lagoon didn't exist before 1935 - it formed as the Breiðamerkurjökull glacier, a tongue of Vatnajökull (Europe's largest ice cap), retreated. Now 284 meters deep (Iceland's deepest lake), it's filled with blue, white, and black icebergs, some composed of ice over 1,000 years old. From the parking area, walk along the western shore to escape the crowds - in 10 minutes you'll be practically alone facing the icebergs. With luck, you'll see seals lounging on the ice.

Just across Route 1, Diamond Beach awaits. Icebergs drifting from the lagoon to sea get tumbled and polished by waves, then wash up on the black sand. The contrast between translucent ice and volcanic sand is striking - come at sunrise or sunset for the best colors. Don't climb on the ice blocks - they can tip over.

activities.text: Operators offer amphibious boat or zodiac excursions on Jökulsárlón lagoon to navigate between icebergs. The zodiac tour allows closer approach to the glacier front - allow about 1 hour. Book in advance in summer, places fill quickly.

practical_info.booking: Advance booking strongly recommended in summer

visits.text: Before leaving Höfn, take time to tour the harbor. This small fishing town of 2,000 inhabitants is the Icelandic lobster capital (humar, actually langoustine). From the docks, the view of Vatnajökull on clear days is spectacular - the ice cap appears to fall directly into the sea.

The road between Höfn and Egilsstaðir crosses the East Fjords, one of Iceland's least visited regions. Route 1 winds between mountains and deep fjords, with fishing villages clinging to the coast. Make regular photo stops - each bend reveals a new panorama. At Djúpivogur, a tiny village of 450 residents, don't miss the series of giant stone eggs along the harbor, reproducing eggs of 34 local bird species.

From Egilsstaðir, make the 27 km detour to Seyðisfjörður, the most beautiful village in the East Fjords. The road descends a mountain pass before plunging toward the fjord's end where a colorful artists' village awaits. Spot the little blue church (Bláa kirkjan) at the end of the main street paved with a rainbow path. The village hosts the ferry to Denmark - if a ship is in port, the atmosphere changes completely.

activities.text: In Egilsstaðir, a short but rewarding hike leads to Fardagafoss waterfall. The trail runs 2 km one way with moderate elevation gain - allow about 30 minutes climbing. The waterfall tumbles into a rocky amphitheater with valley views. Continue a few minutes beyond to reach a second smaller waterfall, even more peaceful.

If you have time, take a drive around Lake Lagarfljót, Iceland's third-largest lake. At 25 km long and 112 meters deep, it harbors - according to legend - the Lagarfljótsormur, a giant serpent cousin to the Loch Ness monster. In 2012, a video showing a serpentine form in the lake went viral worldwide. Even without a monster, the setting is superb, especially when Hallormsstaður forest (Iceland's largest - which says a lot about the country) borders the shore.

visits.text: From Egilsstaðir, take Route 862 to Dettifoss, Europe's most powerful waterfall. Water from the glacial river Jökulsá á Fjöllum - fed by Vatnajökull - plunges 44 meters high and 100 meters wide with an average flow of 200 m³/s, which can double in summer. You'll hear the roar well before seeing it, and the mist is visible for kilometers. Approach from the west bank (Route 862, paved): the trail from parking runs 1.5 km. No barriers separate visitors from the canyon edge - stay careful. This is where Ridley Scott filmed the opening scene of Prometheus.

One kilometer upstream from Dettifoss, don't miss Selfoss. Smaller (11 meters high) but wider, it forms an arc in the canyon - fewer tourists, more tranquility. The trail connecting the two waterfalls follows the Jökulsá á Fjöllum canyon edge, one of Iceland's most spectacular.

If you have time, push on to Ásbyrgi, the horseshoe-shaped canyon 30 km north. At 3.5 km long and 1 km wide, its 100-meter cliffs shelter a birch forest - rare in Iceland. According to Norse mythology, it's the hoofprint of Sleipnir, Odin's eight-legged horse. In reality, the canyon was carved by glacial floods about 10,000 years ago.

Arriving in the Mývatn region, detour to Grjótagjá cave. This small lava cave shelters a hot spring naturally heated by geothermal activity. Game of Thrones fans will recognize it - a famous scene between Jon Snow and Ygritte was filmed here. Bathing has been prohibited since Krafla eruptions in the 1970s raised water temperature above 50°C, but the place merits a visit.

activities.text: Settle in the Mývatn region for the night. If you arrive early enough, drive around the lake (36 km via Route 848) to scout tomorrow's sites. The lake, whose name means 'midge lake,' is Iceland's fourth-largest - in summer, midges feed millions of birds, including 13 breeding duck species. Bring a head net if you're sensitive.

visits.text: Start with Dimmuborgir, the 'dark castles.' This lava field of improbable shapes formed about 2,300 years ago when a lava flow met a lake - steam sculpted pillars, arches, and caves. The Kirkjuhringur trail (2.6 km, easy) passes through Kirkjan cave whose natural vault resembles a Gothic cathedral interior. This is also where scenes at the Fist of the First Men in Game of Thrones were filmed.

Then climb to Hverfjall crater, 15 minutes' walk from Dimmuborgir. This tephra crater formed 2,500 years ago in a single eruption measures 1 km in diameter and 140 meters deep - one of the world's best-preserved circular craters. The ascent is steep (250 m elevation gain, 20-30 minutes) but at the summit, the 360° panorama over Mývatn, lava fields, and Krafla volcanic system is exceptional. The complete rim circuit takes 1 hour.

Head to Hverir (Námaskarð), the geothermal field at the base of Námafjall pass. The ground is covered with bubbling gray and yellow mud pots, fumaroles spewing sulfurous steam - and everything gives off a persistent rotten egg smell. The landscape is entirely barren, not a blade of grass, with hues ranging from ochre to bright red. You'd think you were on another planet. The site is right next to Route 1, free and accessible year-round. Stay on marked trails - the earth's crust is thin and water underneath reaches 100°C.

Push on to Krafla volcano, 20 minutes north. The Víti crater ('hell' in Icelandic) harbors a striking blue-green lake, formed during an eruption in 1724. Krafla's last eruption series, between 1975 and 1984, profoundly transformed the landscape. From the parking area, the trail to Leirhnjúkur lava field crosses still-steaming flows - some less than 50 years old.

activities.text: End this volcanic day with a soak at Mývatn Nature Baths (Jarðböðin). These open-air geothermal baths offer milky blue-green water between 36 and 40°C, with views over the lake and mountains. Less crowded and cheaper than the southern Blue Lagoon, it's the preferred option for travelers who know the country. Open daily until midnight in summer - in June, you can soak under the midnight sun.

visits.text: Leaving Mývatn westward, stop at Goðafoss after 45 minutes' drive. This horseshoe waterfall is 30 meters wide and 12 meters high. Its name - 'waterfall of the gods' - dates to the year 1000: when Iceland officially adopted Christianity, chieftain Þorgeir Ljósvetningagoði supposedly threw his statues of Norse gods into the waterfall to mark the break with paganism. Two parking areas provide access to both banks - take the east bank for the best overall view.

Akureyri, the 'capital of the north,' has only 19,000 inhabitants but serves as a metropolis in northern Iceland. Climb to Akureyrarkirkja, the church dominating downtown. Designed by the same architect as Reykjavik's Hallgrímskirkja (Guðjón Samúelsson), it houses stained glass from Coventry's old cathedral in England, salvaged after its 1940 bombing. From the forecourt, views plunge over Eyjafjörður fjord, Iceland's longest.

Descend to the Lystigarðurinn botanical gardens, the world's northernmost open to the public (at 65°N). Free, they shelter over 7,000 plant species - surprising for this latitude. Open from June to September, it's an unexpected haven of greenery. An on-site café offers cakes and hot drinks overlooking the flowerbeds.

activities.text: Don't leave Akureyri without visiting the municipal pool (Sundlaug Akureyrar). In Iceland, the pool is a social institution - locals gather to chat and relax. Akureyri's features several heated outdoor pools, waterslides, and a steam bath. Entry costs a few hundred króna and it's one of the best ways to live 'Icelandic-style.'

Stroll the main pedestrian street. Amusing detail: Akureyri's traffic lights are heart-shaped - a 2008 initiative during the financial crisis to lift residents' spirits. Take advantage to taste a kleina (Icelandic doughnut) in one of the downtown cafés.

visits.text: The road to Siglufjörður via the Tröllaskagi peninsula is one of Iceland's most beautiful - and least traveled. Take Route 82 along the coast rather than the tunnel: mountains plunge into fjords, isolated farms dot the landscape, and the road passes through single-lane tunnels carved through rock (yield in the recesses provided for this purpose).

Stop at Hofsós for its infinity pool perched on the cliff edge, with direct views over Skagafjörður fjord and Drangey island. The water is 38°C and the pool edge merges with the fjord below - this is probably Iceland's most beautiful pool.

Siglufjörður, at the peninsula's end, is a former fishing village nestled at the end of a fjord ringed by mountains. In the 1940s-50s, it was Iceland's most important herring port - the population had climbed to 10,000 during the 'herring boom.' The Herring Era Museum recounts this period with a reconstructed salting factory and fishing boat. The museum won a European Museum Award - astonishing for a village of 1,200 inhabitants.

activities.text: Enjoy the evening strolling through Siglufjörður. The harbor is surrounded by mountains descending almost vertically into the fjord. In summer, the sun circles the horizon without setting, bathing the village in permanent golden light. A few restaurants serve fresh fish caught that very day.

visits.text: Leaving Siglufjörður westward, detour via the Vatnsnes peninsula to see Hvítserkur. This 15-meter basalt monolith, pierced by two arches, stands in the water a few meters from shore. Its silhouette evokes a rhinoceros lowering its head to drink - Icelanders see it as a troll petrified by the rising sun. Erosion threatened collapse, so its base was reinforced with concrete in 1995. From the parking area, a viewpoint offers a plunging view, but descend to the beach at low tide to approach it. The peninsula is also one of Iceland's best spots for observing seals - watch carefully at Ósar beach.

Continue south and stop at Hraunfossar and Barnafoss waterfalls, 70 km before Borgarnes. Hraunfossar is a series of streams gushing directly from a 900-year-old lava field, over nearly a kilometer - water filtered through porous rock and emerges in dozens of crystalline threads tumbling into the Hvítá river. Just next door, Barnafoss (the 'children's falls') owes its name to a legend: two children supposedly disappeared crossing a natural stone arch spanning the waterfall. The arch has since collapsed, but the name remains.

End the day in Borgarnes. The Settlement Center recounts in two interactive exhibitions the Viking colonization of Iceland and the Saga of Egill, the most famous Icelandic warrior-poet. It's one of the best introductions to Icelandic history you'll find. Allow 1 hour for both exhibitions.

activities.text: If you're a natural hot spring enthusiast, detour to Deildartunguhver, Europe's most powerful hot spring with a flow of 180 liters per second at nearly 100°C. A spa has been developed next door - perfect for relaxing after the long drive from the north.

visits.text: First stop: Kirkjufell, Iceland's most photographed mountain. At 463 meters high, it stands isolated beside the sea, near the fishing village of Grundarfjörður. Its nearly perfect conical shape - sculpted by glaciers - earned it the nickname 'arrowhead mountain' in Game of Thrones (season 7). The best viewpoint is from Kirkjufellsfoss waterfall directly opposite: the three water threads in the foreground with the mountain behind - this is THE Iceland photo. Come early morning or evening to avoid crowds.

Descend to the peninsula's south coast to Arnarstapi. The coastal trail between Arnarstapi and Hellnar (2.5 km, 45 minutes) follows natural arches, sea caves, and basalt columns battered by waves. In summer, the cliffs are covered with nests of kittiwakes and fulmars. At Arnarstapi, don't miss Gatklettur, a spectacular stone arch right at the water's edge.

Continue to Djúpalónssandur, a black pebble beach at the foot of Snæfellsjökull glacier. Four basalt stones of different weights (23, 54, 104, and 154 kg) once served as strength tests for fishermen: only those who lifted at least the third (104 kg) were allowed to embark. You'll also see the rusted remains of a British trawler wrecked here in 1948.

On the return route, stop at Búðakirkja, the little black church of Búðir. Built in 1848, isolated in the middle of a moss-covered lava field, it contrasts strikingly with the landscape. The Snæfellsjökull glacier - the one from Jules Verne's novel 'Journey to the Center of the Earth' (1864) - can be glimpsed at the peninsula's summit when weather is clear.

activities.text: If weather permits, operators offer excursions on Snæfellsjökull glacier by snowmobile or on foot. Snæfellsjökull rises to 1,446 meters and constitutes the entry point for the journey imagined by Jules Verne. The guided climb takes about 4 to 5 hours round trip. Book in advance.

visits.text: Return to Reykjavik. If you haven't yet visited Perlan, now's the time. This glass dome building, perched on six geothermal hot water tanks, houses an interactive museum on Icelandic nature - with a 100-meter artificial ice cave, planetarium, and 360° observation deck offering one of the best views over the city and mountains.

Push through the doors of the National Museum (Þjóðminjasafn Íslands). Its collections cover the country's history from Viking colonization to today. The centerpiece is a 13th-century church door carved with dragons and knights. Allow 1.5 hours for a complete visit.

Explore the Grandi district, the old harbor quarter undergoing transformation. You'll find the maritime museum, art galleries, Icelandic design workshops, and a food court with Icelandic and international cuisine stands. It's Reykjavik's liveliest neighborhood for eating and browsing.

activities.text: For your last evening, treat yourself to a soak in one of Reykjavik's geothermal pools. Laugardalslaug is the city's largest, with a 50-meter pool, several hot pots at different temperatures, and a waterslide. It's the quintessential Reykjavik socializing spot - sink into the hot water and let go after 12 days on the road.

If you prefer a more exclusive setting, geothermal baths have been developed at the ocean's edge minutes from downtown. Water at 38°C facing the North Atlantic, with views of Mount Esja - a memorable way to conclude your Iceland circuit.

seo.h1_title: Iceland Road Trip: The Complete Ring Road in 12 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: Depart from Reykjavik and complete the full circuit of Iceland via the Ring Road in 12 days. From southern waterfalls to eastern fjords, from Mývatn volcanoes to northern fishing villages, this itinerary covers every face of the island - glaciers, black sand beaches, geysers, lava fields, and hot springs.
seo.seo_hook: 1,332 km of Ring Road, dozens of waterfalls, two nights facing Mývatn's volcanoes, the Jökulsárlón glacial lagoon, and the wild Snæfellsnes peninsula.
featured_list: Reykjavik: geothermal capital between Hallgrímskirkja and Harpa
Jökulsárlón: 18 km² glacial lagoon dotted with thousand-year-old icebergs
Mývatn: volcanic lake ringed by craters and steaming lava fields
Snæfellsnes: peninsula crowned by Jules Verne's glacier

places_taglines.IS::reykjavik: Where Nordic design meets steaming geothermal pools under the midnight sun
places_taglines.IS::vik: Black sand beaches beneath the sleeping fury of Katla volcano
places_taglines.IS::hofn: Gateway to glaciers where langoustines meet Europe's largest ice cap
places_taglines.IS::egilsstadir: Where emerald valleys harbor legends of serpents in mountain lakes
places_taglines.IS::myvatn: Where Earth's raw power sculpts craters, mud pots, and eternal steam
places_taglines.IS::akureyri: Northern capital where heart-shaped traffic lights warm Arctic winters
places_taglines.IS::siglufjordur: Herring kingdom nestled where mountains plunge into forgotten fjords
places_taglines.IS::borgarnes: Where Viking sagas echo from Settlement days to modern shores
places_taglines.IS::stykkisholmur: Colorful gateway to a peninsula crowned by Verne's mystical glacier

highlights: Dettifoss: Europe's most powerful waterfall (44 m × 100 m, 200 m³/s)
Reynisfjara: black sand beach ranked among the world's 10 most beautiful non-tropical beaches
Snæfellsjökull: Jules Verne's glacier rising to 1,446 m

itineraries.title: Wild Iceland: Snæfellsnes & Westfjords in 8 Days
itineraries.subtitle: Iceland off the beaten path, from Snæfellsnes volcanoes to Europe's most remote fjords
itineraries.notes: 4x4 vehicle recommended for the Westfjords (gravel roads, mountain passes). Roads accessible June to September only. Last gas station before Látrabjarg is in Flókalundur - fill up there. The Baldur ferry (Stykkishólmur → Brjánslækur) must be booked in advance during summer. Warning: gravel roads in the Westfjords often require speeds of 30-40 km/h. Actual driving times are often much longer than Google Maps suggests. /// WHAT MAKES THIS DIFFERENT: this itinerary does NOT circle the island. It focuses on the Snæfellsnes peninsula and the Westfjords - a region only 10% of visitors discover. No Jökulsárlón, no Mývatn, no south coast. If you want the greatest hits, choose the 12-day Ring Road circuit or the 17-day Highlands route. This circuit is for those who want wild Iceland, the puffin cliffs of Látrabjarg, deserted fjords, and hot springs with nobody around.

visits.text: Arrive at Keflavík and drive toward Reykjavik (50 minutes). Spend the afternoon exploring the city center on foot: climb Hallgrímskirkja (74.5 m, built 1945–1986) for the panoramic view from the bell tower, then head down to Harpa, the glass concert hall inaugurated in 2011 whose 714-panel facade was designed by artist Ólafur Elíasson.

Stroll along Laugavegur, the main shopping street. Continue to the Old Harbour to see the fishing boats and colorful docks. If you arrive early, stop by the National Museum for a quick introduction to the island's Viking history.

activities.text: End the day with a soak in one of the city's geothermal pools. Vesturbæjarlaug in the west district is less touristy than the big ones - water at 38°C, local atmosphere guaranteed.

visits.text: Morning drive northwest. First stop: Hraunfossar waterfalls, where dozens of streams pour directly from a 900-year-old lava field over nearly a kilometer. Right next door, Barnafoss (the "children's waterfall") takes its name from two children said to have disappeared while crossing a natural stone arch over the falls.

Turn onto Route 54 toward the Snæfellsnes peninsula. Stop before Kirkjufell (463 m), Iceland's most photographed mountain, with Kirkjufellsfoss waterfall in the foreground. It's the "arrowhead mountain" from Game of Thrones (season 7). The nearby fishing village of Grundarfjörður has 900 inhabitants.

Head down to Snæfellsnes' south coast. The coastal trail between Arnarstapi and Hellnar (2.5 km, 45 min) runs along natural arches and basalt columns. Don't miss the Gatklettur arch at Arnarstapi. Further along, Djúpalónssandur is a black pebble beach with four test stones that fishermen had to lift to prove their strength (23, 54, 104, and 154 kg).

On your way back up to Stykkishólmur, stop at Búðakirkja, the little black church from 1848 standing alone in a lava field. The Snæfellsjökull glacier (1,446 m) - the one from Jules Verne's "Journey to the Center of the Earth" - dominates the peninsula on clear days.

activities.text: In the evening, explore the colorful harbor of Stykkishólmur. Climb to the Súgandisey lighthouse for views over Breiðafjörður fjord and its thousands of islets. If you still have energy, try the local mussels at one of the harbor restaurants.

visits.text: Board the Baldur ferry at Stykkishólmur (departs 9am in summer). The 2.5-hour crossing passes through Breiðafjörður fjord, dotted with thousands of islets. If you take the first ferry, you can make a stopover on Flatey island - a village of 5 permanent residents where all the houses have been restored in 19th-century style. Cars aren't allowed on the island; a crew member will park yours on the other side. Catch the next ferry to Brjánslækur.

From Brjánslækur, take the road toward the southern Westfjords. Make a detour to Rauðisandur, a 10-km beach of red and golden sand - a striking contrast to Iceland's usual black beaches. The road down is steep and unpaved but manageable. With luck, you'll spot seals on the shore.

activities.text: On the way, stop at Hellulaug, a natural hot spring on the fjord's edge. The small pool carved into the rock offers water at about 38°C with a direct view of mountains and fjord - free and open year-round, with no facilities whatsoever. Bring a towel.

visits.text: A day dedicated to the Látrabjarg cliffs, Europe's westernmost point. These cliffs stretch for 14 km and reach 440 meters high. They shelter the world's largest colony of Atlantic puffins - millions of birds nest here from mid-June to late July. Razorbills, guillemots, fulmars, and kittiwakes complete the spectacle. The puffins aren't shy: lie down on the ground a meter from the edge (no closer!) to photograph them up close. Warning: there are no barriers, the ground is riddled with burrows and can collapse under your weight.

On the way, stop at Breiðavík beach, a stretch of golden sand - rare in Iceland - with turquoise water (but freezing cold). The contrast with the dark cliffs is magnificent. A few isolated farms dot the landscape.

activities.text: In the evening, wander around the small harbor of Patreksfjörður (650 inhabitants). It's one of the few Westfjords villages with services (gas, grocery, restaurants). The fjord is surrounded by mountains and the atmosphere is that of an inhabited end of the world.

visits.text: A spectacular day's drive north through the fjords. The road is long and often gravel - expect 30-40 km/h on some sections, don't underestimate the travel time. The major stop: Dynjandi (also called Fjallfoss), considered by many to be Iceland's most beautiful waterfall. Standing 100 meters tall, it widens from 30 meters at the top to 60 meters at its base - it looks like a bridal veil unfurled down the mountain. It's actually a series of 7 tiered waterfalls, each with its own name. The trail from the parking lot (800 m, 20 minutes) passes the first six before reaching Dynjandi at the top. Protected natural monument since 1981. Come late in the day for the best light - in the morning it's backlit.

Along the way, Arnarfjörður fjord is one of the most beautiful and widest in the Westfjords. Route 60 winds along its shores with constant panoramas of snow-capped mountains and the fjord's calm water. Make photo stops.

If you have time, stop in Þingeyri, a village of 250 inhabitants at the head of Dýrafjörður fjord. It's one of the oldest trading posts in the Westfjords. The Old Blacksmith's Workshop (Svartasmiðjan) has been restored and now serves as a cafe - a surprising spot in the middle of nowhere.

activities.text: The road between fjords crosses mountain passes with dizzying views. Some sections are single-lane roads - yield in the pullouts. Drive slowly, enjoy the scenery, and keep in mind that in the Westfjords, the journey IS the destination.

visits.text: Ísafjörður (2,600 inhabitants) is the "capital" of the Westfjords - the town nestles on a triangular sandbank at the head of Skutulsfjörður fjord, hemmed in by mountains that rise steeply. The old quarter (Neðstikaupstaður) preserves 18th-century wooden houses, among Iceland's oldest. Stroll through the colorful streets and visit the Westfjords Heritage Museum (Vestfjarðasafn) housed in a warehouse from 1784.

Take the excursion to Bolungarvík, 15 minutes away. This fishing village is the northernmost in the Westfjords. The Ósvör maritime museum, a reconstruction of a traditional fishing station with turf huts and fish-drying racks, gives a glimpse of the harsh life of Icelandic fishermen. From the Bolafjall viewpoint (accessible by car), the view over the Arctic Ocean and the Greenland coast on clear days is striking.

On the other side of the fjord, the village of Flateyri (200 inhabitants) deserves a detour. It's the largest village in Önundarfjörður fjord, with a historic bookstore (Gamla bókaverslunin, opened 1914) whose decor has barely changed since. In 1995, a devastating avalanche struck the village, killing 20 people - an imposing protective wall has been built since.

activities.text: If you're adventurous, outfitters in Ísafjörður offer kayak excursions in the fjords - a unique way to see the coast from the water, between cliffs and bird colonies. In summer, boat trips to the Hornstrandir nature reserve (Iceland's wildest and most uninhabited region) are also possible.

visits.text: Long drive southeast along the Strandir coast, one of Iceland's most isolated regions. The desolate, wind-battered landscapes have fueled centuries of witchcraft legends. In Hólmavík, the Museum of Icelandic Sorcery and Witchcraft (Galdrasýning á Ströndum) is as strange as it is fascinating: it traces the 17th-century witchcraft trials in Iceland, where 21 people were burned (including 20 men, unique in Europe). The museum's most famous object is a reconstruction of "nábrók," trousers made from human skin said to bring wealth to the wearer.

Stop at Drangsnes, a tiny fishing village on the Strandir coast. The three hot pots by the sea are free and open 24/7 - you bathe in 40°C water with a direct view of Grímsey island in the fjord. It's one of Iceland's most authentic baths, far from any tourist infrastructure.

activities.text: The Strandir coast is arctic fox territory, Iceland's only native land mammal. Keep your eyes open while driving - they're sometimes spotted along the road, especially early morning. An arctic fox research and rescue center operates in the region.

visits.text: Drive back to Reykjavik via Borgarfjörður. Along the way, stop at Deildartunguhver, Europe's most powerful hot spring: 180 liters of water at 100°C gush out every second. A spa has been built next door for those who want to soak (in cooled water, rest assured).
If you didn't have time to visit Perlan in Reykjavik, this is your last chance. This glass dome perched on geothermal tanks houses a 100-meter artificial ice cave and a 360° panoramic terrace.

activities.text: Enjoy your last evening in Reykjavik. The Grandi district (former industrial harbor) brings together restaurants, galleries, and the food court with Icelandic and international cuisine. A relaxed way to conclude 8 days in Iceland's wildest regions.

seo.h1_title: Wild Iceland: Snæfellsnes & Westfjords in 8 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: Forget the Ring Road and its tour buses. This 8-day itinerary takes you to the Iceland that only 10% of visitors discover: the Snæfellsnes peninsula dominated by Jules Verne's glacier, then the Westfjords, a region of deep fjords, towering cliffs populated by millions of puffins, and fishing villages clinging to the edge of the world.
seo.seo_hook: Látrabjarg cliffs teeming with millions of puffins, Dynjandi waterfall cascading like a bridal veil, wild fjords without tourists, and hot springs on the ocean's edge.
featured_list: Látrabjarg: 14 km of cliffs and the world's largest puffin colony
Dynjandi: 100-meter waterfall in 7 tiers, nicknamed the bridal veil
Ísafjörður: miniature capital of the Westfjords at the head of a mountain-ringed fjord
Kirkjufell: Iceland's most photographed mountain on the Snæfellsnes peninsula

places_taglines.IS::reykjavik: Where colorful streets meet geothermal pools and Nordic creativity
places_taglines.IS::stykkisholmur: Colorful harbor gateway to the Westfjords ferry crossing
places_taglines.IS::patreksfjordur: Where fishing boats meet the world's largest puffin cliffs
places_taglines.IS::isafjordur: A mountain-ringed town perched on a sandbank at the world's edge
places_taglines.IS::holmavik: Where witchcraft legends linger on Iceland's loneliest coast

highlights: Látrabjarg: 14 km of cliffs reaching 440 m, world's largest puffin colony
Dynjandi: 100-meter waterfall in 7 tiers at the head of Arnarfjörður fjord
Baldur ferry: 2.5-hour crossing between Snæfellsnes and Westfjords via Flatey island
Only 10% of Iceland's visitors venture into the Westfjords
